steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The verb "belonged" requires a preposition to form a complete prepositional phrase. Ochus subvittatus is introduced as a formal species identification after the completed clause.

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
The completed clause "knew exactly what species it belonged to" is independent; choice B's colon then introduces the direct identification, Ochus subvittatus. A colon correctly introduces a direct identification after an independent clause.

A — to and: Adding "and" creates "knew exactly what species it belonged to and Ochus subvittatus," which makes the species name a list item rather than an identification — grammatically broken and nonsensical. Fails.

B — to:: "belonged to:" ends the independent clause and uses a colon to introduce the formal species identification. Correct.

C — to;: A semicolon must be followed by another independent clause. "Ochus subvittatus" is a noun phrase, not an independent clause. Fails.

D — to: No punctuation leaves the species name dangling without any signal that it is the answer to "what species" — grammatically ambiguous and stylistically incomplete. Fails.
